questions.q: What is the first step in a system design interview?
What is a load balancer for?
questions.a: To spread traffic across multiple backends.
Splitting data or traffic across multiple partitions.
Keeping copies of data on multiple nodes.

questions.q: What is a cache used for in system design?
questions.a: To reduce latency and backend load.
A control that caps request volume over time.

questions.q: Why do you estimate traffic and storage early?
questions.a: Capacity numbers determine architecture, cost, and failure modes.

questions.q: Why is idempotency important in public APIs?
questions.a: Clients and networks retry, so repeated requests must be safe.

questions.q: Why do pagination and cursors matter?
questions.a: They keep large result sets manageable and stable.
They stop cascading failures by failing fast on unhealthy dependencies.

questions.q: Why do you separate read and write paths sometimes?
questions.a: Because their scaling, consistency, and latency needs can differ.

questions.q: Why does a single hot partition hurt a sharded system?
questions.a: One shard becomes the bottleneck even if the rest are idle.

questions.q: When would you choose a monolith over distributed services?
questions.a: When simplicity, locality, and faster iteration matter more than independent scaling.

questions.q: When would you choose a microservice split?
questions.a: When a boundary has clear ownership and independent scaling or deployment value.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with multi-region active-active systems?
questions.a: Better resilience and locality, but much harder consistency and conflict handling.
It keeps designs grounded in realistic throughput and storage constraints.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with eventual consistency?
questions.a: Higher availability and lower latency, but temporary stale reads or conflicts.

questions.q: Why do you think about failure explicitly?
questions.a: Every real system fails, and the design must define the recovery path.
